calendar.matters:
Notice Of Motion
Off Calendar Apr-12-2018 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Rosailda Perez, Esq. and Robert S. Sandoval, Esq., of the Office of the Attorney General, T:415.510.3506, for petitioner/moving party;
No other appearance.

RULING: There being no objection, the Court adopts the tentative ruling.

PETITIONER DEPARTMENT OF CONSUMER AFFAIRS' PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE is GRANTED. Per Government Code 20026, disability retirement requires a disability of "permanent or extended duration." The Department correctly notes that the ALJ's decision repeatedly and erroneously states that the Department must show a permanent and substantial incapacity. (AR000758; 762; 764; 765.) The lack of permanence appeared to be a basis for the decision (AR000762, par. 16) and the ALJ's references to the controlling statutes and Mansperger v. Public Employees Retirement System, (1970) 6 Cal.App.3d 873, does not cure the error. That error alone is a basis to grant the writ because the ALJ applied the wrong standard of review and the court analyzes that legal issue de novo. (Beckley v. Bd. of Administration (2013) 222 Cal.App.4th 691, 697.) The administrative findings, although afforded a strong presumption of correctness, are contrary to the weight of the evidence. (See Fukuda v. City of Angels, (1999) 20 Cal.4th 805, 817.) "[T]o be 'incapacitated for the performance of duty''means the substantial inability of the applicant to perform his usual duties." (Mansperger, 6 Cal.App.3d at 876.) Dr. Ferranti opined that Mr. Hanson could not perform his essential job duties due to a mental condition. (AR00071-74; 000492; 000536; 000554; 000743.) That testimony is persuasive. The ALJ noted that Dr. Ferranti did not perform an IME, but her examination was tantamount to an IME. (AR000554-555.) Dr. Davies' testimony is not persuasive and is insufficient, when viewed in light of all the evidence, to support the determination that Mr. Hanson is not eligible for a disability retirement. Dr. Davies erroneously concluded that Dr. Ferranti did not diagnose Hanson with a disorder. (AR000670-673; 000197.) Dr. Davies found the lack of a diagnosis "meaningful." (AR000197.) Dr. Davies admitted that Dr. Ferranti had Mr. Hanson's personnel records and "[h]er ability to assess him in multiple spheres was much greater than mine." (Id.) Dr. Davies examined specific job duties rather than Mr. Hanson's usual duties. (AR00046.) Dr. Davies mistakenly concluded that, since Mr. Hanson did not have a medical illness, everything else was irrelevant. (AR000221.) Dr. Davies focused only on the mental illness/diagnosis issue rather than the larger issue of whether Mr. Hanson could perform his usual duties. (AR000437-438.) After exercising its independent judgment and according the ALJ's findings due deference, the court finds Dr. Ferranti's testimony is far more persuasive than Dr. Davies' testimony.

ORDER and JUDGMENT signed in open court.

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: G. Gonzales; Not reported.
